                                                                                                                                 Review Article

                                                                                                           Published: 6 February 2026
                                                                                                             https://doi.org/10.20935/AcadAI8109




Bovine facial recognition through muzzles
Karla Rejane de Andrade Porto1,2 , Joao Vitor de Andrade Porto2,3, *, Fabricio de Lima Weber2,4 , Selmos Luiz Gressler5 ,
Vanessa Aparecida de Moraes Weber1,4
Academic Editors: Vincent Hilaire, Zenglin Xu


Abstract
This review examines the application of neural network architectures for cattle identification and recognition using snout images as the
primary biometric trait, motivated by the anatomical uniqueness and high inter-individual variability of this region. Beyond reporting
performance metrics, the analyzed literature consistently highlights that the discriminative power of bovine facial recognition systems
is strongly associated with specific visual features, including the spatial configuration of nostrils, texture patterns of the muzzle surface,
local curvature of the snout region, and contrast variations under different illumination conditions. A wide range of algorithms and
tools, such as SIFT, SURF, KNN, and deep learning architectures including YOLO-based detectors and ResNet-50 backbones, have
been employed to extract and model these features effectively. The reviewed studies report high recognition performance, with recent
YOLOv8-based approaches achieving accuracies close to or equal to 100% under controlled conditions. Furthermore, the use of transfer
learning, convolutional feature hierarchies, and ensemble classifiers, such as random forests, enhances robustness to pose variation,
noise, and environmental variability. These findings demonstrate that the success of bovine facial and muzzle recognition is primarily
driven by the quality and stability of learned visual features rather than by dataset-specific configurations. As a result, such systems show
strong potential for real-time livestock monitoring, health assessment, disease tracking, and scalable precision livestock management,
with applicability extending to other animal species.


Keywords: cattle recognition, precision livestock management, neural networks, facial recognition, muzzle recognition

Citation: de Andrade Porto KR, de Andrade Porto JV, de Lima Weber F, Gressler SL, de Moraes Weber VA. Bovine facial recognition
through muzzles. Academia AI and Applications 2026;2. https://doi.org/10.20935/AcadAI8109


1. Introduction

for prediction through static models but also as highly integrated            increasingly complex in dense and highly overlapping popula-
components in more dynamic and real-time decision-making pro-                 tions. Machine learning and artificial intelligence have emerged as
cesses. In this context, advances in computer vision have enabled             technological tools with high problem-solving capacity to address
remote sensing across various sectors of society, ranging from                challenges across different domains of captive species cultiva-
biometrics used in facial recognition and security devices to the             tion [3].

Figure 1 • Distribution of the selected articles in the preliminary research across databases and search platforms by binary combination
from 2019 to 2023.


A basic lexicographical analysis was applied to determine word          five distinct subject groups addressing architecture models, recog-
frequency calculations within the study’s focus, including the total    nition approaches, types of technology, biometric applications,
number of words, average frequency, and the number of hapax             and cattle muzzle recognition.
occurrences. This analysis aimed to characterize the vocabulary
and establish a dictionary of reduced forms, identifying active
and supplementary forms applied in each study, thereby enabling
proper grouping.

The results of the statistical analysis of the 39 articles comprising
the textual corpus revealed 412 occurrences of common words and
156 grammatical forms. The graphical representation of the lex-
icography indicated a higher frequency of terms within intervals
above fifty, with one to two overlapping terms. Regarding hapax or
terms occurring only once, a total of one hundred instances were
observed, of which 24.7% were isolated occurrences and 64.10%
were grammatical forms. These were primarily observed within
frequency ranges of 10 to 20, as shown in Figure 2, with an
average of 10.30 lexical occurrences among the terms.

Given the wide variety of terms, it was not possible to establish
an adequate representation based on similarity. All cited terms,
regardless of their frequency, are presented in alphabetical order
in Table 1. Considering the terms “muzzle” and “recognition”
as central to the classes of active, supplementary, or combined
forms, the database comprised 156 grammatical forms, forming
                                                                        Figure 2 • Example of facial segmentation of the animals.

  animal             cattle               equine             identifique        mflops              provide               system
  animality          classification       extraction         identification     microsatellite      radio                 tag
  architecture       CNN                  face               identifier         MLP                 random                task
  artificial         cognitive            farm               image              model               RANSAC                time
  autoencoder        compression          feature            individual         monitor             recognition           tomasi
  automate           compute              field              inspire            muzzle              reduction             tracking
  background         convolution          filter             intelligence       network             representation        transfer
  bag                convolutional        flann              intelligent        neural              saen                  tree
  ballistics         cow                  flow               intermediate       NN                  schlieren             verification
  barcodes           dairy                FLPP               kamphaeng          orient              science               vibrissa
  base               database             force              LBP                owner               SDAE                  vision

3. Results and discussion                                               cattle, muzzle, and learning, as shown in Figure 3. It is also

materials.
Aligned with the proposed objective and aimed at deepening the
understanding of the selected texts, adaptations were made fol-
lowing the guidelines presented by [10] on machine learning and
techniques for cattle identification, resulting in the formulation of
three guiding questions:

   • Which techniques have been employed for animal identifica-
     tion over the past five years?
   • Which Machine Learning and Deep Learning models have been
     applied in livestock farming to identify and recognize animals?
   • Focusing on cattle farming, which methods and evaluation
     metrics are being used to identify and recognize livestock,
     regardless of their location?
                                                                        Figure 3 • Distribution of the most frequent single words.

frequency in the new selection linked to the guiding questions,         The analytical discussion of the selected literature texts was
the most prevalent terms in descending order were identification,       grouped according to method, application, and accuracy for facial

and muzzle recognition of cattle using machine learning architec-            techniques employed in the reviewed studies, will be further clari-
tures and tools. Each study was further categorized into subgroups           fied and cited accordingly in the subsequent discussion. The texts
defined by the technique investigated, as shown in Table 2. How-             will first be presented by examining the foundational principles
ever, for a more rigorous and structured discussion, each term               of each technique and its applications in animal identification,
and each acronym presented in the table, which correspond to the             followed by a detailed explanation of their specific use in cattle

Table 2 • Methodological grouping of machine learning approaches applied to cattle face and muzzle recognition, organized according
to the employed techniques and target applications.
 Method                           Purpose of use                                                                                     References
 CNNs                             Identification of facial features and muzzle with SIFT                                             [12, 13]
 DBN, SDAE, CLAHE                 Identification with few images; Landmark predictor                                                 [14, 15]
 KNN, SIFT, SURF                  Extraction of facial and muzzle features                                                           [12, 16, 17]
 ResNet + PointNet                Robustly identify specific muzzle patterns                                                         [11, 18]
 ResNet50                         Discriminatory features of the face and muzzle                                                     [19]
 SDAE + DNB                       Structure of the image pattern of the muzzle and face, contour and perception of beads and crest   [20]
 SIFT                             Noise or blurred image with data refinement                                                        [21]
 SURF, SIFT, Dense-SIFT + (χ2)    Multilayer identification and outlier removal                                                      [12]
 SURF; MSER                       Recognition in a mixed set of animals                                                              [22]
 SVM                              Large herds with lighting and multiscale variability                                               [6]
 VGG16_BN; Shuffle NetV2          Muzzle printing in field herds                                                                     [23]
 YOLO (v3) + SIFT; ResNetV2       Different image quality and consistency at angles                                                  [24]
 YOLO(v7)                         Real-time capture of animals, varying lighting, nonlinear movement                                 [25]
 YOLOv3 + ResNet50                Use of few images for individual identification of cattle                                          [5]
 YOLOv5n + ConvBlock1             Interesting points and random sample                                                               [26]
 YOLOv8                           Color depth, pimples and dermal projections                                                        [27]


3.1. Study of the technique and animal identification                        more advanced and rigorous processes to achieve precision and
                                                                             reliability. However, the adoption of cutting-edge or advanced
Visual biometrics refers to the identification of individuals based          technologies carries the risk of operational bottlenecks and the
on distinctive visual characteristics and has gained increasing              constant need for specialized expertise. Therefore, the use of so-
relevance in applications involving animal recognition and iden-             lutions that employ reliable and precise markers through simpler
tification, particularly in the context of wildlife conservation and         and lower-cost approaches, which do not require highly special-
endangered species monitoring [28]. Within this framework, the               ized personnel for daily application and use, is highly advanta-
concept of a “face” is understood as an identity-bearing rep-                geous.
resentation grounded in phenotypic similarity. Etymologically,
the term rostrum, derived from Latin, encompasses the frontal                Unique patterns and discriminative body markings used for indi-
anatomical structures of the head that constitute the facial region          vidual identification are already well established for manta rays,
in mammals, birds, and insects. The presence and morphological               penguins, whales, sharks, and the stripe patterns of tigers and
configuration of structures such as muzzles and beaks enable                 zebras. Based on this knowledge, adaptations have been made for
the categorization of animal groups and the discrimination of                the biometric recognition of chimpanzees, lemurs, cattle, sheep,
intra-group variability based on unique anatomical traits [29].              horses, and pigs [6]. Detection and recognition approaches are
                                                                             particularly important when monitoring species that inhabit hard-
Illustrative examples include the pronounced facial differences              to-reach areas, and their effectiveness can be enhanced through
observed between dog breeds such as bulldogs and German shep-                the use of satellite imagery for detecting, identifying, and counting
herds, as well as between Shih Tzus and Yorkshire Terriers. In               elusive species, whether of medium or large size.
veterinary and livestock management contexts, biometric marker
systems support more efficient identification, monitoring, and               Studies conducted by [31] explored biometric identification using
traceability, contributing to vaccination control, disease manage-           technologies previously applied to human recognition, such as
ment, and the mitigation of theft and fraud. Among the biometric             fingerprint, facial, and retinal pattern analysis, now adapted for
traits commonly explored in animal identification studies are the            animal identification. The muzzle has shown promising results as
iris, retina, muzzle, and body patterns [30].                                a primary biometric feature for recognizing mammals, including
                                                                             cattle, goats, sheep, and horses, providing not only a relevant ID
It is worth noting that the more sensitive a biometric marker is,            for tracking but also additional information for disease control,
the greater the requirement for technologies that rely on more               monitoring of breeding and management programs, as well as
expensive equipment and systems, which directly correlates with              enhancing animal welfare and sustainable production efficiency.
cost. Such technologies demand higher-quality components and                 For identification, object detection was performed using Yolo (v7),

mark points of interest employed SIFT (Scale-Invariant Feature          als even with few images. An autoencoder was adopted to allow the
Transform), followed by FLANN (Fast Library for Approximate             Base-Learner to learn general features by combining supervised
Nearest Neighbors).                                                     and unsupervised approaches. The proposed localization model
                                                                        achieved a mean Average Precision (mAP) of 99.5% and an iden-
The system set demonstrated precise recognition capabilities and
                                                                        tification accuracy of 90.43% with only five samples per animal.
potential applications in animal security and health management.

to as THDD (Horse Dataset). However, the presence of noise              mal mapping. The study recommends the application of trained

methods such as wool marks, tattoos, ear notches, and plastic or
                                                                        veloped for human identification, accuracy was adapted for the
metal ear tags, which increase costs, particularly when applied to
                                                                        problem of cattle recognition. Using a deep learning approach,
large herds of sheep, goats, pigs, and cattle [30].

Consequently, regarded as a pioneering milestone in computer            livestock or prevent insurance fraud. The replacement of OSNet’s
vision, biometrics combined with general animal monitoring in           convolutional layers with depthwise separable convolutions and

identification, and verification of animals [19].                       maintaining the same experimental conditions. This modification

signed based on computer vision and deep learning, facilitating
the localization, tracking, and identification of animals through       3.2. Application of the technique in cattle farming
the direct application or combination of multiple approaches. De-
                                                                        Historically, livestock management technologies have progressed
tection can be implemented using techniques such as Histograms
                                                                        from predominantly manual identification procedures to auto-
of Oriented Gradients (HOGs), boosted cascades, and particle
                                                                        mated systems enabled by machine learning, allowing individual
filters, alongside object tracking algorithms and Kalman filters.
                                                                        cattle recognition based on body contours, facial features, and
Among machine learning methods, Local Binary Patterns (LBPs)
                                                                        muzzle patterns. Building upon architectural adaptations such as
are used for classification, Fisher Linear Projection Preservation
                                                                        those applied to OSNet, recent studies have demonstrated that
(FLPP) for facial and muzzle recognition, and Support Vector
                                                                        deep learning frameworks can be effectively tailored to cattle
Machines (SVMs) for classification. Regarding architectures, R-
                                                                        identification without compromising computational efficiency.
CNN and YOLO are widely applied due to their speed, along with
RetinaNet and detectors such as DetectoRS, SOLOv2, DETR, and            In dairy production systems, these recognition technologies fur-
Mask R-CNN for processing [32].                                         ther extend beyond identity verification, supporting the assess-
                                                                        ment of movement behavior, water and feed intake, rumination
Feature extraction, being a process used to obtain a unique char-
                                                                        patterns, estrus detection, and the early identification of health
acteristic of the object in focus, allows for noise correction and
                                                                        conditions, including lameness and mastitis. Within this con-
the enhancement of results. Among the unique identifier pat-
                                                                        text, architectures and tools such as ArcFace (Additive Angular
terns used in studies with animals having few images, several
                                                                        Margin Loss), RetinaFace-Mobilenet, and Cattle-FaceNet are fre-
general-purpose extraction algorithms can be employed, such as
                                                                        quently reported. Additionally, the application of convolutional
SIFT, SURF, and BRISK (Binary Keypoints). For scalability, ORB
                                                                        neural networks, including Res52Net with task-specific adapta-
(FAST-oriented and BRIEF-rotated) can also be applied [28].
                                                                        tions, achieved recognition rates of 91.3% at a processing speed
Based on this premise, Xu et al. [14] proposed a method for the         of 24 images per second, which increased to 94.53% through the
identification of cows using a small number of images as a measure      integration of GPN features with the core ResNet50 architecture,
to reduce the costs of herd recognition and localization for 243        illustrating the balance between accuracy gains and real-time
animals. The approach employed a YOLOv5 detector augmented              applicability [12].

Continuing with the findings of [22], recognition accuracy values     method to maximize inter-class dispersion of muzzle point images
exceeded the benchmarks, reaching 95.9% and 97.4% when using          while efficiently minimizing intra-class dispersion through the
three layers of the YOLOv3 algorithm. For muzzle print feature        application of a muzzle point image scatter matrix. The effec-
extraction, SIFT combined with RANSAC (Random Sample Con-             tiveness of the proposed recognition method reached 96.87% for
sensus) was employed to remove outliers and enhance robust-           individual cattle identification, with a processing time of 10.25
ness. Local Binary Pattern (LBP) was also tested for extracting       s for registration and recognition of the muzzle point, thereby
muzzle print features, achieving an accuracy of 93.3%, and when       creating a biometric database for cattle from diverse sources.
combined with classifiers such as SVM (Support Vector Machine)
                                                                      The bovine muzzle exhibits unique tissue characteristics, being a
and KNN (K-Nearest Neighbor), particularly in the combination
                                                                      moist, thick, hairless surface with grooves and elevations specific
of KNN with SIFT and SURF, recognition accuracy reached 99.5%.

Comparing the reported recognition and accuracy percentages           ture, known as vibrissae, consists of keratinized hair extensions
for convolutional networks Res52Net and ResNet50, and subse-          that function as sensory organs in some animals. Common among
quently for YOLOv3 combined with the RANSAC extractor and             mammals and arranged around the muzzle, the complex or sinus
classifiers including LBP, SVM, KNN, SIFT, and SURF in dairy          hair follicle structure enables animals to assess the size of an
cattle herds, these values were superior to those produced by         object or determine the texture of a surface. Different species
Multilayer Perceptron (MLP), Random Forest (RF), and Decision         display variations in the shape and structure of vibrissae, although
Tree (DT) algorithms applied to the same sample, which exhibited      some properties are shared, such as the ability to function as a
lower recognition rates of 69.32%, 74.88%, and 79.60%, respec-        multicomponent force/torque sensor [34].
tively, highlighting the superiority of convolutional networks.
                                                                      The presence of prominences on bovine muzzles has been docu-
It is undeniable that the introduction of ICT resources leads to      mented in studies involving image capture and machine learning,
effective management of agricultural production in terms of effi-     with results validated through comparison and qualitative and
ciency, accessibility, and scalability. Reports by [19] demonstrate   quantitative analysis techniques [19]. However, despite advance-
that the use of technology, despite initial resistance to innova-     ments and future prospects highlighted by experimental and sci-
tion in the field, represents a low-cost framework for sanitary       entific studies, the widespread application of muzzle-based recog-
monitoring of cattle, based on behavioral activity images and         nition techniques remains limited due to the relationship between
muzzle patterns. The technique involves Support Vector Machines       spatiotemporal evolution and sensitive measurement caused by
and Decision Tree classification for incremental decision-making      density.
and acceleration, aiming to mitigate noise, enhance contrasts,
                                                                      To address this limitation, Abdellaoui et al. [35] suggests im-
and improve quality. In other words, it is evident that to extract
                                                                      plementing a quantitative flow visualization method based on
image details, a recognition system using machine learning and
                                                                      Background-Oriented Schlieren (BOS). This technique calculates
computer vision achieves higher accuracy for classification and
                                                                      gradients of different densities by analyzing the apparent displace-
identification, utilizing images sourced from servers containing
                                                                      ment of the background pattern for each structure present in the
encrypted muzzle databases, field databases, and health sensor
                                                                      images; thus, BOS was used to validate the digital identity of each
databases.
                                                                      studied bovine.
The accuracy for recognizing cow movement behavior captured
                                                                      Although the use of chips or microchips for cattle detection and
in natural environments using automated devices, smartphones,
                                                                      tracking is a more common practice, it depends on IoT-enabled
and other mobile equipment reached 95.00% when employing
                                                                      wireless systems and is costly for large-scale animal identifica-
the VGG16 network for feature extraction and the Bi-LSTM
                                                                      tion and tracking. Similarly, the implementation of frameworks
(Bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory) classification model
                                                                      also requires hardware integration with the recognition system,
to reduce environmental interference. When applying four algo-
                                                                      trained personnel, and semi-automatic recognition systems based
rithms (C3D, VGG16-LSTM, ResNet50-LSTM, and DenseNet169-
                                                                      on muzzle images. Manual capture results in low similarity match-
LSTM), the average accuracy for movement behavior recognition
                                                                      ing, achieving only 60% accuracy, and despite being a time-
was 97.87%. To mitigate the trade-off between recognition rate
                                                                      consuming process with image database processing, it is possible
and computational complexity, the use of an E3D (Efficient 3D
                                                                      to perform fusion methods, such as combining facial and muzzle
CNN) algorithm was proposed. Compared with the enhanced
                                                                      characteristics. For extracting multi-characteristics of the face
ResNeXt network, ACTION-Net, and C3D-ConvLSTM, the latter
                                                                      and muzzle in images, techniques such as SURF, SIFT, Dense-
was considered the most suitable for deployment on mobile de-
                                                                      SIFT+(x2), Eigen-face with PCA, and LDA were used, providing
vices [31].
                                                                      improved identification accuracy by delivering data related to the
Systems designed for mobile devices should be implemented with        shape and structure of the muzzle and face image patterns, con-
tools to minimize the trade-off between recognition accuracy and      tour features, and texture information. The authors further state
computational complexity due to the use of more efficient algo-       that the availability of these resources and the fusion of multi-
rithms and neural network architectures. Employing quantization       characteristics achieve 98.56% accuracy in identification [19].
techniques to reduce the numerical precision of model weights or
                                                                      While the use of tags or microchips for cattle detection and track-
pruning to remove less important connections or neurons makes
                                                                      ing is effective, these systems rely on IoT-enabled wireless net-
the model more compact and faster, while incurring minimal loss
                                                                      works and become economically unfeasible for large-scale identi-
of accuracy.
                                                                      fication and monitoring. Furthermore, their implementation re-
Motivated by the goal of optimizing technology transfer for           quires the integration of dedicated hardware with recognition
biometric muzzle recognition using surveillance cameras and           systems, as well as trained personnel to operate semi-automatic
wireless network technology, Kumar and Singh [11] proposed a          models based on muzzle images. Manual image capture, in turn,




ACADEMIA AI AND APPLICATIONS                 2026, 2                                                                              7 of 13
                                                                                                     https://doi.org/10.20935/AcadAI8109


yields low similarity rates, typically achieving only about 60%         complex, costly, and time-consuming. In contrast, convolutional
accuracy, while also being a time-consuming process.                    neural networks provide statistical validation for identification
                                                                        applications through deep learning, operating in real time, even
A challenge for integrating ICT with livestock management in
                                                                        though challenges may arise due to occlusion, nonlinear external
cattle recognition and in using various methods to identify pri-
                                                                        image variations, or color similarity between animals [25].
mary muzzle features is the availability of access to Bluetooth,
WiFi, and RFID tools for more modern techniques. For tradi-             Among the machine learning models aimed at cattle identifica-
tional methods, access is often limited to online resources on          tion, commonly applied methods include SVM (Support Vector
some farms. Advanced technological resources, in terms of field         Machine), KNN (K-Nearest Neighbor), ANN (Artificial Neural
accuracy and performance, require approaches for feature ex-            Network), CNN (Convolutional Neural Network), ResNet (Resid-
traction, animal classification, and robust algorithms to achieve       ual Network), Inception, YOLO, and Faster R-CNN to character-
optimal results. The classification algorithms cited include logistic   ize cattle coat patterns. These are complemented by LBP (Local
regression, Naive Bayes, Decision Trees, K-Nearest Neighbors,           Binary Pattern) and robust feature extraction techniques such
Support Vector Machine algorithms, random forests, and ker-             as SURF, scale-invariant SIFT, Inception or CNN-based feature
nel methods. Superior results were achieved by applying SIFT,           extraction, ResNet and Inception, and YOLO for detection. When
SURF, ORB (FAST-oriented and rotated BRIEF), and XGBoost                used in combination, ResNet and VGG stand out by integrating
algorithms [36].                                                        three different detection models: YOLO, Faster R-CNN, and Mask
Continuing the studies by [12], the CORF3D contour map test             R-CNN [10].
was introduced to suppress environmental noise, resulting in an         Experimental results based on a two-branch Convolutional Neu-
accuracy of 99.64% for cattle recognition data. This represented a      ral Network (TB-CNN) with images captured from different an-
significant improvement over the 83.35% accuracy observed when          gles showed that the features from the two branches were fused
using the Weber Local Descriptor (WLD) alone, and 96.5% after           through a global average pooling layer, combined with individual
fusion with LBP and SVM. By combining a multi-level structure           cattle classification. This fusion of feature extraction networks
with the Inception ResNetV2 model, recognition accuracy in-             aims to enhance the extraction capability, while the pooling layer
creased to 98.21% for Holstein cattle. Additionally, the combina-       improves classification by reducing the number of network pa-
tion of FAST + SIFT + FLANN for body pattern recognition across         rameters [22].
528 segments achieved an accuracy of 96.72%. Although multiple
experimental setups produced accuracies above 90% with various          The authors report that the recognition rates of the TB-CNN
algorithm combinations, only the use of the CORF3D contour map          model reached 99.85% for Simmental cattle, 99.81% for Hol-
enabled results approaching 100%.                                       stein cow faces, and 99.71% for a mixed dataset of cattle and
                                                                        cows. The parameterization allowed a comparison of the TB-CNN
A study involving 4923 muzzle images collected from 268 beef
                                                                        with other conventional networks, such as ResNet, which has
cattle was evaluated using two classification models, ResNet50

and VGG16BN, with a maximum accuracy of 99.5% achieved using

ResNet50. Approaches employing biometric identification sys-
                                                                        TB-CNN with these less common networks produced recognition
tems with convolutional neural networks (CNNs) or Deep Belief
                                                                        rates of 99.70%, 98.07%, and 97.96%, respectively, with corre-
Networks (DBNs) also demonstrated high accuracy, although they

required large datasets and significant computational resources.

                                                                        it enhanced the interaction between feature maps and categories,
geNet models. In this case, quantized and compressed data were

generated to reduce data volume and redundancy. However, the
inclusion of the discrete cosine transform (DCT) in the ResNet50        Overall, the experiments demonstrated high identification and ex-
model resulted in an accuracy of 99.4%, surpassing that of the          traction rates, all above 97%, and the combinations increased the
VGG16BN model [16]. The use of multiple models operating in             robustness of the technique while reducing image capture noise
tandem for identification tasks is a common practice to address         for secure recognition. Notably, the three-part structure facilitated
scalability challenges associated with recognition methodologies,       compression, excitation, and reweighting, allowing for feature re-
though the choice of model is always guided by the algorithm that       calibration and the learning of global information, while enabling
provides the best results while maintaining flexibility to operate      interdependence among channels. By emphasizing the most im-
alongside other systems to minimize noise and errors.                   portant features of each channel, less significant features were sup-
                                                                        pressed, resulting in more efficient recognition outcomes.
Using a similarity scoring measure through Decision Tree clas-
sification, the matching and classification of structural features      Biometric systems based on visual muzzle recognition have been
and the presence of vibrissae on cattle muzzles were performed,         applied due to their ability to identify individual animals without
demonstrating an efficiency of 97.99% for the unique identifica-        the need for invasive techniques, while also providing improved
tion of each animal [30].                                               protection and management across different species. This approach
                                                                        enables the classification of animals and their various categories,
Techniques involving muzzle points have been employed to iden-
                                                                        facilitating the study of populations within their natural habitats.
tify cattle with high recognition accuracy using deep learning.

and profiles in breeding animals, specifically cattle. While DNA        enable analysis, tracking, and record-keeping for verification and
analysis can indicate an individual’s profile through the pres-         parentage assignment for each animal, particularly those intended

regardless of the animals’ angles or positions. Therefore, it can be

stated that all classes achieved a high number of correct identi-

fications, and none of the proposed models showed statistically

significant differences among them. ANOVA hypothesis testing in

RStudio with p-values < 0.05 was used for each metric, indicating

that the models are reliable for cattle recognition.

Subsequently, using the Shi-Tomasi detector for cattle identifica-              and muzzle identification, body and breed pattern recognition,
tion and the SIFT and SURF feature descriptors to extract features              and bovine facial analysis.


Table 3 • Comparison of algorithmic systems applied to cattle muzzle recognition with reported accuracy above 95%.
  Method                               Precision       Processing speed    Scenario                                             Quantity of animals (average)

  YOLO (You Only Look Once)            99.43–99.7%     High Velocity       Large herds with variable lighting conditions and    268
                                                                           multi-scale features

  SVM (Support Vector Machine)         96.74–100%      Moderate            Identification of facial and muzzle features, com-   Varied
                                                                           bined with SIFT

  Convolutional Neural Network (CNN)   99.5–99.85%     Moderate            Classification of muzzle and facial features using   243
                                                                           images captured from different angles

  Faster R-CNN                         95–98.21%       Moderate and Slow   Identification of body and facial patterns in con-   Varied
                                                                           trolled environments

  ResNet50                             99.5%           Moderate and Slow   Facial and muzzle feature extraction with large-     4923
                                                                           scale datasets

  KNN + SIFT + SURF                    96.5–99.5%      Moderate            Identification of specific muzzle patterns with      Varied
                                                                           robustness

4. Conclusions                                                         Mato Grosso do Sul (UEMS), the National Council for Scientific
                                                                       and Technological Development (CNPq), and Kerow Precision
This review systematically assessed neural network-based archi-        Solutions.
tectures for cattle identification and recognition, with emphasis
on facial and muzzle regions due to their distinctive and stable
anatomical characteristics. The surveyed literature demonstrates
                                                                       Author contributions
that both classical feature-based methods, such as SIFT, SURF,         Conceptualization, K.R.d.A.P. and J.V.d.A.P.; methodology,
and KNN, and deep learning architectures from the YOLO and             K.R.d.A.P. and J.V.d.A.P.; validation, V.A.d.M.W. and F.d.L.W.;
ResNet families are capable of extracting discriminative repre-        formal analysis, K.R.d.A.P., F.d.L.W. and S.L.G.; investiga-
sentations for individual identification, with convolutional neu-      tion, K.R.d.A.P. and J.V.d.A.P.; resources, V.A.d.M.W. and
ral networks consistently forming the core of high-performing          F.d.L.W.; data curation, K.R.d.A.P.; writing—original draft
systems and achieving recognition accuracies above 90%, and            preparation, K.R.d.A.P. and J.V.d.A.P.; writing—review and
near-optimal results in controlled scenarios, as reported for recent   editing, K.R.d.A.P., J.V.d.A.P. and S.L.G.; visualization, J.V.d.A.P.
YOLOv8 implementations.                                                and F.d.L.W.; supervision, V.A.d.M.W. and F.d.L.W.; project
Importantly, a critical synthesis of the reviewed studies reveals      administration, V.A.d.M.W. and F.d.L.W.; funding acquisition,
that robust bovine facial and muzzle recognition depends primar-       V.A.d.M.W. and F.d.L.W. All authors have read and agreed to the
ily on a consistent set of visual characteristics, including fine-     published version of the manuscript.
grained texture patterns of the muzzle surface, the spatial con-
figuration of nostrils, local curvature cues, and hierarchical fea-    Conflict of interest
ture representations learned by deep convolutional layers. These
characteristics emerge recurrently across different architectures      Karla Rejane de Andrade Porto was affiliated with Kerow Preci-
and application contexts, indicating that feature relevance, rather    sion Solutions as a Technological Development scholarship holder
than model selection alone, is central to system performance. This     funded by CNPq. Joao Vitor de Andrade Porto is affiliated with
insight directly addresses the need for more substantive findings      Kerow Precision Solutions as a collaborator and member of the
by moving beyond accuracy reporting toward an understanding of         IA2/CNPq project. Fabricio de Lima Weber is the Executive Di-
which visual cues underpin successful recognition.                     rector and a researcher at Kerow Precision Solutions. Selmos Luiz
                                                                       Gressler is the Executive Director and a researcher at Agron Inter-
Building on this analysis, future research should explicitly in-       net Services Provider Ltd. The affiliated companies and funding
vestigate the contribution of individual visual features within        bodies had no role in the conception, design, data acquisition,
architectures such as YOLOv8 and ResNet-based models, either           analysis, interpretation of results, writing of the manuscript, or
through targeted ablation studies or structured feature attribution    in the decision to submit the work for publication. The authors
analyses, using available datasets. In parallel, the development of    declare that there are no other financial or personal relationships
computationally efficient and interpretable models, coupled with       that could be construed as a potential conflict of interest.
more standardized evaluation protocols, is essential to facilitate
practical adoption. Addressing these directions will bridge the gap
between methodological advances and real-world deployment,             Data availability statement
enabling the effective integration of intelligent vision-based sys-
                                                                       All data supporting the findings of this publication are available
tems into routine livestock management and supporting scalable
                                                                       within this article and/or its supplementary materials.
innovation in precision agriculture.
